attributes.stem:
A 29-year-old woman at 30 weeks of pregnancy presents to the Emergency Department with right-sided flank pain and nausea. She is writhing in pain and unable to find a comfortable position. She has vomited twice. She has no significant medical history and takes only pregnancy vitamins.

On examination, her pulse rate is 90/min, blood pressure 115/75 mmHg, respiratory rate 18/min, and temperature 36.8°C. Urine dipstick shows blood 1+, otherwise unremarkable. An ultrasound reveals a 3 mm stone in the distal ureter with no evidence of hydronephrosis.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate management?
options: Medical expulsive therapy with alpha blockers
attributes.explanation:
This pregnant patient has presented with renal colic, and the likely culprit identified as a small distal ureteric calculus. Urolithiasis is among the most frequent non-obstetric reasons for which pregnant patients are admitted to hospital. This reflects the overall commonness of stones; their incidence is actually unchanged by pregnancy. However, diagnosis can be more challenging in the pregnant population due to significant overlap between symptoms of physiological hydronephrosis in pregnancy and of renal colic, plus the low sensitivity of ultrasound versus CT imaging.

This patient has a small distal stone, with no evidence of obstruction, pyelonephritis, or systemic sepsis. In this setting, conservative management with analgesia and hydration is recommended in the first instance. This approach is supported by observations that spontaneous passage of stones <1cm approaches 80% in pregnancy, likely due to progesterone-mediated relaxation of ureteric smooth muscle causing physiological dilatation of the ureter.

Medical expulsive therapy, including alpha blockers, is generally controversial regardless of pregnancy status and guidelines vary on their use. No clear benefit has been shown in pregnant patients, and it is worth noting that tamsulosin (the typical alpha blocker used in this setting) is not covered by the PBS and the upfront cost must be discussed in the shared decision-making process. In this case, the stone is very much expected to pass spontaneously, and whether alpha blockade will cause any further dilation of the ureter in the presence of progesterone (as mentioned above) is questionable.

Surgical management options range from stent insertion, percutaneous nephrostomy or ureteroscopic stone retrieval. These options are second-line and should be made as part of a multidisciplinary team approach to care in cases where pain is unmanageable, infection has set in, or there is bilateral obstruction (or unilateral obstruction in a patient with one kidney).

Extracorporeal shockwave lithotripsy is contraindicated in pregnancy and has been associated with miscarriage, fetal growth restriction, and placental displacement.

There is no role for empirical antibiotics.
learningPoints: Conservative management is first-line for small distal ureteric stones in pregnant women
Spontaneous passage of stones <1cm approaches 80% in pregnancy due to progesterone-mediated ureteric smooth muscle relaxation
Surgical intervention is reserved for cases with unmanageable pain, infection, or significant obstruction
references: White J, Ory J, Lantz Powers AG, Ordon M, Kroft J, Cox A. Urological issues in pregnancy: A review for urologists. Can Urol Assoc J. 2020 Oct;14(10):352-357. PMCID: PMC7716830.
Semins MJ, Matlaga BR. Kidney stones during pregnancy. Nature Reviews Urology. 2014;11(3):163-168.
